questions.text: Magnetic field will not produce in case of?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because A positive charge, top, moving perpendicularly through a magnetic field is deflected. The right-hand rule, bottom, predicts the direction in which the positive charge will be deflected. A similar left-hand rule predicts the deflection of negative charges.

This option is incorrect because A negative charge introduces a negative sign, which flips the direction of the force. So, for a negative charge your right hand lies to you, and the force on the negative charge will be opposite to the direction indicated by your right hand.

This option is correct because Magnetic fields will not be produced in case of neutral particles. The protons, cathode rays and alpha particles are charged particles, so the magnetic field deflects them. But there is no charge on the neutrons, so they are not deflected by the magnetic field.
This option is incorrect because Option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Correct option is C. Magnetic fields will not be produced in case of neutral particles. The protons, cathode rays and alpha particles are charged particles, so the magnetic field deflects them. But there is no charge on the neutrons, so they are not deflected by the magnetic field.

questions.text: Two long parallel wires Wire 1 and Wire 2 repel each other as shown in the figure. What could be the reasons?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Both carry current in same direction
options.explanation: They will attract each other in this case.

options.text: Both carry current in opposite direction
options.explanation: Two parallel wires have currents travelling in opposite directions, the magnetic fields generated by those currents between the wires will both point in the same direction (use right hand grip rule). Thus, wires would repel each other.

options.text: Wire 1 has current, but Wire 2 has no current
options.explanation: They repel each other because both carry current in opposite directions.

options.text: Wire 2 has current, Wire 1 has no current
options.explanation: They repel each other because both carry current in opposite directions.

questions.explanation: Two parallel wires have currents travelling in opposite directions, the magnetic fields generated by those currents between the wires will both point in the same direction (use right hand grip rule). Thus, wires would repel each other.

questions.text: Two parallel straight conductors, in which current is flowing in the same direction, attract each other. The cause of it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them. This happens because:Magnetic Fields Around the Conductors:Each current-carrying conductor generates a circular magnetic field around it, as described by Ampère's right-hand rule.Magnetic Interaction:The magnetic field of one conductor exerts a force on the second conductor and vice versa.When currents are in the same direction, the magnetic fields between the wires reinforce each other in a way that causes an attractive force.Lorentz Force Explanation:The moving charges (current) in one conductor experience a force due to the magnetic field created by the other conductor.According to the Lorentz force law, this force acts inwards, pulling the conductors together.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.
Two parallel straight conductors carrying current in the same direction attract each other due to the magnetic force between them.

questions.explanation: Correct option is 'A'.

options.text: Nature of the material of the conducting wire

The direction of the magnetic lines of force does not depend upon the Area of the conducting wire.
The direction of the magnetic lines of force does not depend upon the Amount of the current.
The direction of the magnetic force depends on the direction of the current and the direction of the magnetic field.

questions.explanation: The direction of the magnetic force depends on the direction of the current and the direction of the magnetic field. Of these two, the direction of the current is in options so that will be the correct answer.

questions.text: A unit of induction is said to exist at a point where the force per unit charge experienced by a positive test charge, moving with a velocity of 1ms-1 in the direction perpendicular to the field is 1 Newton. What is this phenomenon called?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A gravitational field is a region of space around a mass where another mass experiences a force of attraction. It is unrelated to the force experienced by a charge moving perpendicularly to a magnetic field.

The magnetic field is a region around a magnetic material or a moving electric charge within which the force of magnetism acts. A magnetic field is a force field that is created by moving electric charges and magnetic dipoles and exerts a force on other nearby moving charges and magnetic dipoles.
Magnetic flux is the measure of the number of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, not directly related to the force experienced by a moving charge.
Flux generally refers to the rate of flow of a property per unit area, and is not specific to the magnetic force experienced by a moving charge.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Magnetic Field. A magnetic field is defined by the force it exerts on moving charges. In this question, the force per unit charge experienced by a test charge moving perpendicularly to the field indicates the presence of a magnetic field. The force is proportional to the velocity of the charge and the strength of the magnetic field.Option A (Gravitational Field) is incorrect because it deals with forces due to gravity, not magnetic forces.Option C (Magnetic Flux) is incorrect because it describes the number of magnetic field lines passing through a surface, not the force on a moving charge.Option D (Flux) is too general and does not specifically relate to magnetic fields affecting moving charges.

questions.text: A wire carries a current out of the page. Which diagram below correctly describes the magnetic field of the wire?

options.explanation: This figure represents the electric field of a positive charge.
Figure B represents the electric field of a negative charge.
This shows the direction of the magnetic field around a wire carrying current directed into the page.
This represents the magnetic field around a current carrying wire with current being directed out of the page.
Magnetic field between flat, opposite poles of magnets can be represented this way.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the wire. If you wrap your right hand’s fingers around the wire with your thumb pointing in the direction of the current, then the direction in which the fingers curl will give the direction of the magnetic field. As the current is directed out of the page, then the magnetic field will have a counter clockwise direction. So “D” is the correct answer.

questions.text: If a wire having current 10A has a 3T magnetic field, what will be the magnetic field at a point from the wire at double the distance?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Any current-carrying wire is surrounded by the magnetic field which is: B = µ0I/2πr It can be seen from the above equation that the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the distance and directly proportional to the current. When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.
When distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.
When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.
When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will become half, which is 1.5 T.

questions.explanation: Any current-carrying wire is surrounded by the magnetic field which is B = µ0I/2πr. It can be seen from the above equation that the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the distance and directly proportional to the current. When the distance from the wire becomes double, the magnetic field will reduce by a factor of two, which is 1.5 T.

questions.text: Two long straight parallel wires held vertically have equal but opposite currents as shown in the figure. Which of the following effects will be observed?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Magnetic field at ‘X’ is stronger than that at ‘Y’ and ‘Z’
Magnetic field at ‘X’ is weaker than that at ‘Y’ and ‘Z’
Magnetic field at ‘X’, ‘Y’ and ‘Z’ is same
Magnetic field at ‘X’ is weaker than that at ‘Y’ but stronger than that at ‘Z’.
Magnetic field at ‘Y’ is stronger than that at ‘X’ and ‘Z’
options.explanation: When two parallel wires carry equal and opposite currents, they create a magnetic field around them. The direction of the magnetic field at any point is given by the right-hand rule. Now, let's consider the magnetic field at points X, Y, and Z. At point Y, the magnetic fields created by the two wires are in the same direction and add up, resulting in a stronger magnetic field than at either X or Z.At point X, the magnetic field due to the current in the left wire is in the upward direction, and the magnetic field due to the current in the right wire is in the downward direction. These two magnetic fields oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.At point Z, the magnetic field due to the current in the right wire is in the upward direction, and the magnetic field due to the current in the left wire is in the downward direction. These two magnetic fields also oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.Therefore, the magnetic field at Y is stronger than that at X and Z.

questions.explanation: When two parallel wires carry equal and opposite currents, they create a magnetic field around them. The direction of the magnetic field at any point is given by the right-hand rule. Now, let's consider the magnetic field at points X, Y, and Z. At point Y, the magnetic fields created by the two wires are in the same direction and add up, resulting in a stronger magnetic field than at either X or Z.At point X, the magnetic field due to the current in the left wire is in the upward direction, and the magnetic field due to the current in the right wire is in the downward direction. These two magnetic fields oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.At point Z, the magnetic field due to the current in the right wire is in the upward direction, and the magnetic field due to the current in the left wire is in the downward direction. These two magnetic fields also oppose each other, resulting in a weaker magnetic field than at point Y.Therefore, the magnetic field at Y is stronger than that at X and Z.

questions.text: Magnetic field is set up in a region around a conductor, carrying:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charges.

questions.explanation: When charges are in motion, they have current (AC, DC or pulsating DC); magnetic field is produced around a current carrying conductor due to the flow of these charge.andMagnetic field is created whenever an electric current flows through a conductor. This is because a moving electrical charge creates a magnetic field. The strength and direction of the magnetic field depend on the amount of current flowing and the direction of flow. Magnetic fields can also be created by permanent magnets (bar magnets). These are made from materials that are naturally good conductors of electricity, such as iron. The molecules in these materials line up in rows, which creates a strong magnetic field. When you bring a conductor close to a magnet, the magnetic field from the magnet affects the moving charges in the conductor. This causes current flow, creating a current-carrying conductor, which creates its own magnetic field. These two fields interact with each other, resulting in a force that either attracts or repels the objects.

If the current flows in straight parallel wires in opposite direction, then the magnetic field in their middle region will reinforce each other (repulsion).
They will indeed affect each other so this is wrong.
As both these options are correct so this is also correct.

questions.explanation: If the current flows in straight parallel wires in same direction, then the megnetic field in their middle region tend to cancel each other (attraction). if the current flows in straight parallel wires in opposite direction, then the magnetic field in their middle region will reinforce each other (repulsion).Two Current Carrying ConductorsWhen two wires carrying a current are placed parallel to each other, their magnetic fields will interact, resulting in a force acting between the wires. The magnitude of the force acting on each wire is equal, but the directions are opposite. This is true even if the conductors carry currents of different magnitudes.The diagram below illustrates two examples where the direction of the magnetic field around each wire is drawn with the • × notation. Diagram A shows the current in the wires travelling in the same direction and diagram B shows the current travelling in opposite directions:

questions.text: Magnetic field lines around a current carrying wire are drawn in clockwise direction on a page. The current is flowing:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is correct as per the right-hand rule; the magnetic field lines curl in a clockwise direction when the current is flowing into the page.
This option is incorrect. If the magnetic field lines are clockwise, the current cannot be flowing out of the page.
This option is incorrect. The current does not flow to the left side when the magnetic field is drawn clockwise.
This option is incorrect. For a clockwise magnetic field, the current cannot be directed to the right side of the page.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Into the page'. According to the right-hand rule, if you point your thumb in the direction of the current (which is assumed to be flowing into the page), your curled fingers will point in the direction of the magnetic field lines. Since the question states that the magnetic field lines are drawn in a clockwise direction, this indicates that the current must be directed into the page.Options 'Out of the page', 'Left side of the page', and 'Right side of the page' are all incorrect because they do not align with the right-hand rule's prediction for a clockwise magnetic field. If the current were flowing out of the page, the magnetic field would be counterclockwise, not clockwise.

questions.text: The magnetic field produced due to the current in a straight wire is proportional to the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: B = μ0I / (2πr)The amount of current flowing through the conductor: The more current that flows through the conductor, the stronger the magnetic field will be.

The length of the wire does not affect the magnetic field. However,it is often confused by,the inverse of the distance from the wire: The closer you are to the wire, the stronger the magnetic field will be.
The permeability of free space: This is a constant that describes the ability of a material to conduct magnetic fields. This factor is constant for each material.

No, the magnetic field produced due to the current in a straight wire is not affected by the diameter of the wire. The magnetic field is only affected by the current and the distance from the wire.The reason for this is that the magnetic field is produced by the moving electrons in the wire. The diameter of the wire only affects the cross-sectional area of the wire, which does not affect the number of electrons moving through the wire. The number of electrons moving through the wire is determined by the current, so the magnetic field is only affected by the current.In other words, if you have two wires with the same current but different diameters, the magnetic field will be the same for both wires. The only difference will be that the magnetic field will be concentrated closer to the wire with the smaller diameter.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below: The magnetic field is proportional to the current and inversely proportional to the distance from the wire. B = μ0I / (2πr)where:B is the magnitude of the magnetic fieldμ0 is the permeability of free spaceI is the current in the wirer is the distance from the wire

questions.text: The direction of the magnetic lines of force depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Nature of the material of the conducting wire
options.explanation: The nature of the material only affects the strength of the magnetic field, not the direction. For example, a copper wire will produce a weaker magnetic field than an iron wire, but the direction of the magnetic field will be the same in both cases.

The area of the conductor only affects the strength of the magnetic field, not the direction. For example, a thicker wire will produce a stronger magnetic field than a thinner wire, but the direction of the magnetic field will be the same in both cases.

The amount of the current only affects the strength of the magnetic field, not the direction. For example, a wire carrying a larger current will produce a stronger magnetic field than a wire carrying a smaller current, but the direction of the magnetic field will be the same in both cases.

The direction of the magnetic lines of force can be found using the right-hand rule. If you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, your fingers will curl in the direction of the magnetic field lines.

questions.explanation: The direction of the magnetic lines of force can be found using the right-hand rule. If you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, your fingers will curl in the direction of the magnetic field lines.

questions.text: The direction of magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force can be taken along:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The normal to the magnetic field at a point is perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point. However, the magnetic field lines are tangent to the magnetic field at each point, so the normal to the magnetic field is not the same as the tangent to the magnetic field lines.

options.text: Axis of the magnetic line of force at that point
options.explanation: The axis of the magnetic line of force is the line along which the magnetic field lines are oriented. However, the axis of the magnetic line of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point.

The direction of magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force can be taken along the tangent at that point. This is because the magnetic field lines are tangent to the magnetic field at each point. The normal to the magnetic field at a point is perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point, and the axis of the magnetic line of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the magnetic field at that point.The direction of the magnetic field at a point can also be determined using the right-hand rule. If you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field, your palm will be pointing in the direction of the magnetic force.

options.text: Perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid
options.explanation: The axis of a solenoid is the central axis of the solenoid. The magnetic field inside a solenoid is parallel to the axis of the solenoid, so the direction of the magnetic field at a point on the magnetic lines of force is not necessarily perpendicular to the axis of the solenoid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C. The direction of the magnetic field at a point on magnetic lines of force is along the tangent to the field line at that point. This is because magnetic field lines represent the path along which a north pole would move, and at any given point, this motion is tangential to the curve of the field line.Option A is incorrect because the normal to a point is perpendicular to the field line and does not indicate the direction of the magnetic field.Option B is incorrect because the axis of the magnetic line of force is a conceptual line and does not necessarily indicate the direction of the magnetic field at every point.Option D is incorrect because the magnetic field within a solenoid is parallel to its axis, not perpendicular.

questions.text: A solenoid 15cm long has 300 turns of wire. A current of 5A flows through it. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid?

options.explanation: As per the calculation (refer to the explanation below), the correct answer is 4πx10-3. Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per the calculation (refer to the explanation below), the correct answer is 4πx10-3. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The magnetic field inside a solenoid can be calculated using the formula:B = µ0 x n x Iwhereµ0 = permittivity of free space = 4πx10-7I = Current = 5An = is the number of turns per unit length (turns per meter) = 300/0.15 = 2000Substitute these values in the formulaB = 4πx10-7 x 5 x 2000B = 4πx10-7 x 1x104B = 4πx10-3
As per the calculation (refer to the explanation below), the correct answer is 4πx10-3. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: Two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length. If both these currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per calculations.

When the two parallel long wires carry the same current and repel each other with a force F per unit length we have:F₁ / ℓ = I² B μ₀ / (2 π h)And when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes:F₂ / ℓ = (2I)² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = 4 I² B μ₀ / (2 π (3h))F₂ / ℓ = (4/3) I² B μ₀ / (2 π h) = (4/3) F₁ / ℓTherefore, when the currents are doubled and the wire separation tripled, the force per unit length becomes 4/3 of the initial force per unit length

questions.text: Which graph correctly gives the magnitude of the magnetic field outside an infinitely long straight current carrying wire as a function of the distance "r" from the wire?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.
Please refer to the main explanation box provided below.

questions.explanation: For an infinitely long straight wire carrying current I, the magnitude of the magnetic field (B) at a distance "r" from the wire is inversely proportional to the distance "r." Mathematically, it follows the following relationship:B ∝ 1/rThis means that as you move farther away from the wire, the magnitude of the magnetic field decreases. The graph of the magnetic field magnitude (B) versus the distance "r" will show a hyperbolic shape. As "r" increases, B decreases, and the graph approaches zero asymptotically.In summary, the graph should look like a hyperbola with the magnetic field magnitude decreasing as you move away from the wire along the horizontal axis (distance "r") and approaching zero as the distance increases.

questions.text: If compass needles are placed on a card board along a circle, with the center at the wire, they will point in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Compass needles point in the N-S direction because the Earth itself is a magnet. The Earth's magnetic field is strongest at the north and south poles, and weakest at the equator. The compass needle is a magnet, and it aligns itself with the Earth's magnetic field. The north pole of the compass needle points towards the south magnetic pole of the Earth, and the south pole of the compass needle points towards the north magnetic pole of the Earth.
The Earth's magnetic field is not aligned with the east-west direction.
The Earth's magnetic field is not aligned with the north-east direction.
The Earth's magnetic field is not aligned with the west-south direction.

questions.explanation: The magnetic compass will point in N-S direction.Compass needles point in the N-S direction because the Earth itself is a magnet. The Earth's magnetic field is strongest at the north and south poles, and weakest at the equator. The compass needle is a magnet, and it aligns itself with the Earth's magnetic field. The north pole of the compass needle points towards the south magnetic pole of the Earth, and the south pole of the compass needle points towards the north magnetic pole of the Earth.

questions.text: Current I is flowing in a long conducting wire, the megnetic induction at a distance r from it is 0.4 tesla, then its value at double the distance will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.
So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.

The magnetic induction at a distance r from a current-carrying wire is inversely proportional to the distance. So, if the distance is doubled, the magnetic induction will be 1/2 as much. In this case, the magnetic induction at double the distance will be 0.4 / 2 = 0.2 tesla.Here is the formula for the magnetic induction at a distance r from a current-carrying wire: B = μ0 * I / (2π * r)
So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.

questions.explanation: So, when distance becomes double "B" will reduce to half.

questions.text: A long straight wire carrying a current of 30 A is placed in an external uniform magnetic field of induction 4 × 10–4 T. The magnetic field is acting parallel to the direction of the current. The magnitude of the resultant magnetic induction in tesla at a point 2.0 cm away from the wire is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Not correct,The formula is giving us a different answer.
Not correct,The formula is giving us a different answer.

Magnetic field due to wireB = μ₀I / 2πr = (4π × 10⁻⁷) / 2π × 30 / (2 × 10⁻²) = 3 × 10⁻⁴ TThis magnetic field will be perpendicular to external magnetic field.∴ Net magnetic fieldB = √(B² + B₀²) = √[(3 × 10⁻⁴)² + (4 × 10⁻⁴)²] = 5 × 10⁻⁴ T
Not correct,The formula is giving us a different answer.

questions.text: Two long parallel straight wires carry equal currents in opposite directions. At a point midway between the wires, the magnetic field they produce is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: ZeroExplanation:When two long parallel straight wires carry equal currents in opposite directions, the magnetic fields they produce at a point midway between them cancel each other out.According to the right-hand rule, the magnetic field produced by each wire at the midpoint will be in opposite directions, and since the currents are equal, the magnitudes of the fields will also be equal.As a result, the net magnetic field at the midpoint will be zero. This is because the fields from the two wires will vectorially add to zero at that point.So, at a point midway between the wires, the magnetic field they produce is zero.

options.explanation: Refer to main explanation box.

options.explanation: The right-hand thumb rule was not invented by Oersted.So , this is not a correct option.
The right-hand thumb rule was not invented by Maxwell.So , this is not a correct option.
The right-hand thumb rule was not invented by Einstein .So , this is not a correct option.
The right-hand thumb rule was invented by British physicist John Ambrose Fleming in the late 19th century for use in electromagnetism.

questions.text: Currents changing with time will produce _ field.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option refers to fields created by stationary charges, which do not change with time. Therefore, it is not applicable to the context of changing currents.

A magnetic field is produced by a steady current; however, the question specifically mentions 'changing currents,' which implies a more complex interaction and the generation of an electromagnetic field.
This is the correct answer because changing currents create electromagnetic fields, which are dynamic fields that vary with time and encompass both electric and magnetic components.
This option is incorrect because there is a valid answer among the provided choices, specifically the electromagnetic field produced by changing currents.

questions.explanation: When a current changes over time, it generates an electromagnetic field, which combines both electric and magnetic fields that vary together. This is a fundamental principle of electromagnetism described by Maxwell's equations. Option A is incorrect because electrostatic fields result from stationary charges, not changing currents. Option B is also incorrect, as it refers to magnetic fields created by steady currents rather than changing ones. Option D is incorrect because it suggests that no correct answer exists among the options given, which is not true since 'Electromagnetic' is indeed the correct choice.

questions.text: A long straight current-carrying conductor has current directed from bottom to top when held vertically. What will be the direction of magnetic field lines when observed from below the conductor?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. According to the right-hand rule, if you position your thumb in the direction of the current (upwards), your fingers will curl in a clockwise direction when viewed from below.
This option is incorrect. If the current flows from bottom to top, the magnetic field lines do not curl in an anti-clockwise direction when viewed from below.
This option is incorrect. The magnetic field lines do not travel vertically upward; instead, they circulate around the wire.
This option is incorrect. The magnetic field lines do not move directly downward; they are oriented in a circular pattern around the wire.

questions.explanation: The magnetic field generated by a long straight current-carrying conductor can be determined using the right-hand rule. When the current flows from bottom to top, if you point your right thumb in the direction of the current, your fingers will curl around the wire. When viewed from below, the fingers will curl in a clockwise direction, indicating that the magnetic field lines are oriented clockwise. The other options are incorrect because they do not represent the actual behavior of magnetic field lines around a straight current-carrying wire.
hints: current directed from bottom to top

questions.text: An electric charge in uniform motion produces:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An electric charge in uniform motion produces an electric field around it. An electric field is a region in space where another charged particle will experience an electric force.
An electric charge in uniform motion produces a magnetic field around it. A magnetic field is a region in space where a moving charged particle will experience a magnetic force.

This option is correct. When an electric charge is in uniform motion, it generates both an electric field and a magnetic field simultaneously. The presence of a magnetic field is due to the motion of the electric charge.
: This option is not correct. A moving electric charge does indeed produce both electric and magnetic fields.

questions.explanation: An electric charge produces an electric field around it, and when the electric charge is in motion it causes a change in position of the electric field. This changing electric field allows the production of a changing magnetic field .Therefore there will be both magnetic and electric fields present. However, if the electric charge was stationary there would be no change in the electric field hence no change in the magnetic field.Electric charge needs to be in motion to allow production of both electric and magnetic fields.

questions.text: The net magnetic field created by the electrons within an atom is due to the field created by their _ motion.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: a) Orbital: Electrons in an atom have orbital motion as they move around the nucleus in specific energy levels or shells. This orbital motion does not contribute to the net magnetic field of the atom.

Electrons have negative charge. When a charge spins, a magnetic field is created around it. Hence the spinning of electrons produces a magnetic dipole. If the majority of electrons in the atom spins in the same direction, a strong magnetic field is produced. The direction of the electron spin determines the direction of the magnetic field. (B)
this option is incorrect.
d) Orbital X spin: This notation suggests a combination of orbital and spin, but it's not the conventional way to represent their combined effect.

questions.explanation: Electrons have negative charge. When a charge spins, a magnetic field is created around it. Hence the spinning of electrons produces a magnetic dipole. If the majority of electrons in the atom spins in the same direction, a strong magnetic field is produced. The direction of the electron spin determines the direction of the magnetic field. (B)

questions.text: The value of permeability of free space μo is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct value of the permeability of free space (μo). It is a constant used in electromagnetism to relate magnetic field strength to the current producing it.
This option has incorrect powers of 10 and units for permeability, which should reflect the relationship between magnetic field and current.
While the numerical value is correct, the units are incorrect. The correct units should be WbA-1m-1.
This option has incorrect numerical value and incorrect units for permeability. The correct unit should match the dimensions of permeability.

questions.explanation: Explanation: The value of permeability of free space μo is 4π × 10-7 WbA-1m-1. It is a physical constant that relates the strength of a magnetic field to the current producing it, and it is also used to define the unit of magnetic flux density, the Tesla.

options.explanation: It is incorrect, as cutting a magnet results in two smaller magnets, each with its own magnetic field. The strength of the magnetic field may be weaker in each piece compared to the original magnet.

It is correct, as when a magnet is cut into two pieces, each piece will still have its own magnetic field. The process of cutting a magnet does not eliminate its magnetic properties. Instead, it creates two smaller magnets, each with a north pole and a south pole.Therefore, cutting a magnet results in two smaller magnets, each with its own magnetic field. questions.text: A moving charged particle is surrounded by?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A moving charged particle is influenced by more than just one field.
A moving charged particle is surrounded by three fields:Electric Field: Due to the charge itself.Magnetic Field: Generated by the motion of the charge.Gravitational Field: Due to its mass.
Although two fields (electric and magnetic) are prominent, the gravitational field is also present, making it three fields in total.
No additional fields are created beyond the electric, magnetic, and gravitational fields. Thus, it is not surrounded by four fields.

questions.explanation: A moving charged particle is surrounded by three fields:Electric Field: This field arises due to the charge of the particle itself and affects other charges in its vicinity.Magnetic Field: Generated due to the movement of the charge. The interaction between charge and motion creates this field.Gravitational Field: The mass of the particle generates a gravitational field, albeit usually very weak compared to the other two fields.Option A (1 field) is incorrect because it ignores the presence of the magnetic and gravitational fields. Option C (2 fields) is incorrect as it overlooks the gravitational field. Option D (4 fields) is incorrect because there isn't an additional field present beyond the electric, magnetic, and gravitational fields mentioned.
